critique.summary: The article claims that Cloudflare has flagged the domain archive.today as associated with command and control (C&C) or botnet activity and that it no longer resolves via Cloudflare's 1.1.1.2 DNS service. However, the provided content does not contain any substantive information or evidence supporting these claims.
mainPoints: archive.today no longer resolves via Cloudflare's 1.1.1.2 DNS resolver.
critique.truthfulness: The article content is insufficient to verify the claims made in the title. The text shown is a Cloudflare security verification page, which does not provide any evidence or confirmation that archive.today is flagged as malicious or that it is blocked on 1.1.1.2. Independent verification from Cloudflare's official threat intelligence or DNS filtering lists is necessary to confirm these claims. Without such evidence, the claims remain unsubstantiated.
considerations: archive.today is a popular web archiving service often used for preserving web pages.
Cloudflare's 1.1.1.2 DNS service is designed to block malware and adult content, but blocking decisions are subject to change and should be verified with official sources.
False positives in DNS-based blocking can occur, so confirmation from multiple sources is important.
The article does not provide any sources or data to support the claim.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers on Cloudflare flagging archive.today as a command and control (C&C) botnet and blocking it via their DNS service 1.1.1.2. Commenters provide context about ongoing DDoS attacks linked to archive.today, possible FBI investigations, and the technical and privacy challenges involved in DNS resolution and blocking.

comments.author: winkelmann
comments.text:
"archive.today is currently categorized as: * CIPA Filter * Reference * Command and Control & Botnet * DNS Tunneling"

Ditto for their other domains like archive.is and archive.ph

Example DoH request:

$ curl -s "https://1.1.1.2/dns-query?name=archive.is&type=A" -H "accept: application/dns-json"

{"Status":0,"TC":false,"RD":true,"RA":true,"AD":false,"CD":false,"Question":[{"name":"archive.is","type":1}],"Answer":[{"name":"archive.is","type":1,"TTL":60,"data":"0.0.0.0"}],"Comment":["EDE(16): Censored"]}

---

Relevant HN discussions:

https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=46843805 "Archive.today is directing a DDoS attack against my blog"

https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=47092006 "Wikipedia deprecates Archive.today, starts removing archive links"

https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=46624740 "Ask HN: Weird archive.today behavior?" - Post about the script used to execute the denial-of-service attack

Wikipedia page on deprecating and replacing archive.today links:

https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wikipedia:Archive.today_guidan...
comments.analysis: This comment provides detailed evidence from Cloudflare's DNS blocking and references multiple relevant Hacker News discussions and Wikipedia guidance, making it highly informative and accurate.

comments.author: rollulus
comments.text:
I think there are two angles to look at this. Yes, there’s the attack on the weblog. But there’s also pressure on archive.today, e.g. an FBI investigation [1] and some entity using fictitious CSAM allegations [2].

[1]: https://arstechnica.com/tech-policy/2025/11/fbi-subpoena-tri...

[2]: https://adguard-dns.io/en/blog/archive-today-adguard-dns-blo...
comments.analysis: This comment adds important context about legal and investigative pressures on archive.today, supported by external links, enhancing the understanding of the situation.

comments.author: kmfrk
comments.text:
What a crazy timeline this has been.

(1) May 04 2019: "Tell HN: Archive.is inaccessible via Cloudflare DNS (1.1.1.1)" [https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=19828317]

    eastdakota on May 4, 2019 on: Tell HN: Archive.is inaccessible via Cloudflare DNS...

    [Via https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=19828702]
    
    We don’t block archive.is or any other domain via 1.1.1.1. Doing so, we believe, would violate the integrity of DNS and the privacy and security promises we made to our users when we launched the service.
   
    Archive.is’s authoritative DNS servers return bad results to 1.1.1.1 when we query them. I’ve proposed we just fix it on our end but our team, quite rightly, said that too would violate the integrity of DNS and the privacy and security promises we made to our users when we launched the service.
   
    The archive.is owner has explained that he returns bad results to us because we don’t pass along the EDNS subnet information. This information leaks information about a requester’s IP and, in turn, sacrifices the privacy of users. This is especially problematic as we work to encrypt more DNS traffic since the request from Resolver to Authoritative DNS is typically unencrypted. We’re aware of real world examples where nationstate actors have monitored EDNS subnet information to track individuals, which was part of the motivation for the privacy and security policies of 1.1.1.1.
    
    EDNS IP subsets can be used to better geolocate responses for services that use DNS-based load balancing. However, 1.1.1.1 is delivered across Cloudflare’s entire network that today spans 180 cities. We publish the geolocation information of the IPs that we query from. That allows any network with less density than we have to properly return DNS-targeted results. For a relatively small operator like archive.is, there would be no loss in geo load balancing fidelity relying on the location of the Cloudflare PoP in lieu of EDNS IP subnets.
    
    We are working with the small number of networks with a higher network/ISP density than Cloudflare (e.g., Netflix, Facebook, Google/YouTube) to come up with an EDNS IP Subnet alternative that gets them the information they need for geolocation targeting without risking user privacy and security. Those conversations have been productive and are ongoing. If archive.is has suggestions along these lines, we’d be happy to consider them.
(2) Sep 11 2021: "Does Cloudflare's 1.1.1.1 DNS Block Archive.is? (2019) (jarv.is)" [https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=28495204]
comments.analysis: The timeline and explanation of DNS privacy concerns provide valuable background on the technical and policy challenges influencing Cloudflare's decisions.

comments.author: jeremie_strand
comments.text: The DNS tuneling flag alongside C&C/botnet is the odd one — that category implies data exfiltration or firewall bypass, not just aggressive crawling or DDoS behavior. Would be interesting to know what traffic pattern triggered it.
comments.analysis: This comment critically examines the classification categories, highlighting potential inconsistencies and suggesting deeper investigation is needed.

comments.author: f-serif
comments.text:
A bit context if you are confused why Public DNS server blocking websites. 1.1.1.2 is Malware blocking DNS server similar to AdBlock DNS server. It is not 1.1.1.1 and 1.0.0.1

Here is the DDoS context https://gyrovague.com
comments.analysis: This comment clarifies the role of the DNS server involved, helping readers understand the technical environment of the blocking.

comments.author: stuffoverflow
comments.text: Archive.today's attack on https://gyrovague.com is still on-going btw. It started just over two months ago. Some IPs get through normally but for example finnish residential IPs get stuck on endless captchas. The JS snippet that starts spamming gyrovague appears after solving the first captcha.
comments.analysis: This provides a firsthand report of the alleged attack behavior, adding practical evidence to the discussion.

comments.author: PeterStuer
comments.text: Otoh, without archive.today a substantial % of HN posts would be unreadable for nearly all of the audience.
comments.analysis: Highlights the importance of archive.today for content preservation on Hacker News, emphasizing the impact of blocking it.

comments.author: razingeden
comments.text:
Cloudflare dns has gone back and forth on whether it wants to resolve them since 2019. It’s taken that away and restored it again (intentionally? mistake?) at least four times.

The c&c/botnet designation would seem to be new though.
comments.analysis: This comment provides historical context on Cloudflare's inconsistent handling of archive.today domains.

comments.author: GTP
comments.text:
I reported the miscalssification, you can do it as well from the linked page.

Edit: reading some comments here seems that I was too fast, and that the story is much more complicated. Having just the Cloudflare page as a context, I assumed the news were a miscalssification. Could someone share more context on what is going on here?
comments.analysis: Shows a cautious approach to the issue, admitting uncertainty and seeking additional information, which is constructive.

comments.author: bunbun69
comments.text: Good. What archive.today is doing is illegal
comments.analysis: This comment expresses a strong opinion without providing evidence or nuance, making it less informative.
